title: How to balance your radiators for an even, cheaper heat
description: Balance your radiators with a lockshield key and a cheap thermometer: even heat in every room, a lower flow temperature and around £60 to £130 a year saved.
blurb: A two-hour free job that evens out hot and cold rooms and trims your gas bill.
lede: If the radiator in your hallway is too hot to touch while the back bedroom never gets past lukewarm, your heating system is unbalanced. Hot water from the boiler takes the easiest route, so the radiators closest to the pump grab more than their share and the ones at the far end of the circuit live off the leftovers. Most people fix the symptom by turning the thermostat up or running the heating longer, and both of those cost real money. Balancing fixes the cause. It needs no new parts, no plumber and no draining down - just a small spanner, a cheap thermometer and a couple of hours on a cold day.
short_answer: Balancing means adjusting the lockshield valve on each radiator so hot water is shared evenly around the whole system. It is free apart from a £15 to £25 thermometer, takes two to three hours, and the even heat it gives you makes two real savings possible: a lower boiler flow temperature (roughly 6 to 9% off heating gas) and a lower thermostat setting (up to 10% per degree). For a typical gas-heated home that is in the region of £60 to £130 a year at current cap rates.

sections.h2: What an unbalanced system is quietly costing you
sections.html:
Water flowing round a heating circuit behaves like traffic: it takes the path of least resistance. The radiators nearest the pump offer the shortest, easiest loop, so they get a strong flow of hot water and heat up fast. By the time the water reaches the furthest radiator there is less flow and less heat left, so that room warms slowly or never quite gets there. That single imbalance drives several expensive habits.

  • The thermostat creeps up. If the room with the thermostat is one of the cold ones, every other room overheats before it is satisfied. If the thermostat sits in a warm hallway, the boiler switches off while the far rooms are still cold, which is one of the classic reasons a house feels cold with the heating on.
  • The heating runs longer. You extend the timer to drag the slow rooms up to temperature, paying to overheat the fast ones the whole time.
  • Plug-in heaters appear. Topping up a cold room with electricity at 26.11p per kWh instead of gas at 7.33p per kWh is heating at more than three times the unit price.
  • Your condensing boiler stops condensing. Radiators taking too much flow return water to the boiler nearly as hot as it left. Condensing boilers only hit their advertised efficiency when the return is below about 55°C, so an unbalanced system can knock a working boiler from around 90% efficiency down into the low 80s.

Balancing restricts the flow through the greedy radiators so the rest of the system gets a fair share. Every radiator then heats at roughly the same rate, and the whole heating system runs cooler, quieter and cheaper.

sections.h2: What you need before you start
sections.html:
The kit list is short and cheap, and you may already own most of it.

  • A lockshield key or small adjustable spanner. The lockshield is the valve at the opposite end of the radiator from the one you normally turn. It hides under a push-on plastic cap and usually has a plain spindle that needs a key or spanner rather than fingers.
  • A way to measure pipe temperature. Two clip-on pipe thermometers (sold as a pair for balancing) or a single digital probe or infrared thermometer. Budget £15 to £25. You can balance by touch alone, but the thermometer method is faster and far more accurate.
  • A notepad. You will be counting valve turns and you will not remember them.

Two jobs are worth doing first. Bleed every radiator, because trapped air ruins the readings and balancing a radiator that is half full of air is wasted effort - the full method is in our guide to bleeding radiators. Then identify your valves: one end of each radiator has either a thermostatic radiator valve (TRV, with numbers on it) or a plain manual wheelhead, and the other end has the lockshield. The lockshield is the one you will be adjusting. The method below works on any wet central heating system - combi, system or conventional boiler, and heat pumps too.

sections.h2: Step by step: how to balance your radiators
sections.html:
Pick a cold day so the heating will run continuously, and allow two to three hours for an average house.

1. Switch off and open everything

Turn the heating off and let the radiators go cold. Open every valve on every radiator fully: TRVs to their maximum setting, wheelheads fully anticlockwise, and every lockshield fully open (cap off, turn anticlockwise until it stops, counting the turns as you go and noting them down).

2. Find the heat-up order

Turn the heating on and walk the house, feeling the top of each radiator. Write down the order in which they get hot. The first to heat are the ones nearest the pump; the last is the end of the circuit. This order is your work list.

3. Balance the fastest radiator first

Go to the first radiator on your list. Close its lockshield fully, then open it a quarter to half a turn. Clip one thermometer to the flow pipe (the hot one feeding the radiator) and one to the return pipe (the cooler one leaving it). For a gas boiler you are aiming for a temperature drop of about 12°C across the radiator - for example 70°C in, 58°C out. Opening the lockshield increases flow and shrinks the drop; closing it restricts flow and widens the drop. Adjust in small steps and give the reading a few minutes to settle after each change.

4. Work down the list

Repeat along the heat-up order. Each radiator further from the pump will need its lockshield a little more open than the last, and the final radiator on the circuit usually ends up fully open or close to it. That is normal: it needs no restriction because everything before it has been throttled back.

5. Settle and recheck

Let the system run for half an hour, then go round once more with the thermometer. Earlier adjustments shift the flow everywhere else, so one quick second pass tightens the whole job up. Note the final lockshield positions in turns from closed, so you can restore them if a valve gets knocked or a radiator is removed for decorating.

No thermometer? Use the cruder by-feel version: adjust the lockshields until every radiator warms up at roughly the same rate from a cold start. It will not be perfect, but it beats an unbalanced system.

Balancing on its own does not meter less gas the moment you tighten the last valve. The saving comes from what it makes possible. A balanced system returns cooler water to the boiler, which keeps a condensing boiler in condensing mode, and it removes the cold-room problem that forces people to run the heating hot and long. That opens two doors.

First, you can drop the boiler flow temperature to around 60°C without anyone noticing, because the heat is now going where it should. Independent testing of typical UK homes puts the saving from that change at roughly 6 to 9% of heating gas. Second, with every room reaching temperature together, most households find they can lower the thermostat by a degree, worth up to 10% of space heating on its own.

Change balancing makes possibleTypical gas savedSaving per year
Lower the boiler flow temperature from 75°C to 60°CAround 8% of heating gasAbout £59
Turn the room thermostat down 1°CUp to 10% of heating gasAbout £73
Both togetherAround 17% combinedAbout £125
Retire a 2kW plug-in heater used 2 hours a day in winterSwaps electricity for gasAbout £90 over a heating season

Based on a home using 10,000 kWh a year of gas for space heating, at 7.33p per kWh gas and 26.11p per kWh electricity, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Checked June 2026. Your figures scale with your own usage.

Against an outlay of £25 at most, the payback is measured in weeks, which puts balancing in the same bracket as bleeding and draught-proofing among the best-value jobs in home heating.

sections.h2: Heat pumps and low-temperature systems: balancing matters even more
sections.html:
Everything above gets more important, not less, as flow temperatures fall. A heat pump runs its radiators at perhaps 35 to 50°C rather than a boiler's 60 to 75°C, so there is far less spare heat to paper over a bad distribution. An unbalanced heat pump system leaves some rooms genuinely cold and pushes the owner to raise the flow temperature, which directly cuts the heat pump's efficiency and raises the electricity bill.

Two differences if you are balancing for a heat pump or running a boiler at low flow temperatures:

  • Aim for a smaller temperature drop. Heat pump systems are typically designed for a drop of around 5°C across each radiator rather than the 12°C used for gas boilers. Check your commissioning paperwork for the design figure.
  • Expect the installer to have done it - and verify. Balancing is part of a proper heat pump commissioning, but it is also one of the most commonly skimped steps. If some rooms lag badly behind others, rebalancing is the first thing to check before touching the flow temperature.

The same logic applies if you have already turned a gas boiler down to 55 or 60°C: at low flow temperatures, balancing is the difference between that change working comfortably and the family vetoing it within a week.

Balancing is the cheapest meaningful upgrade most UK heating systems will ever get. For the price of a pipe thermometer and an afternoon, every room heats at the same rate, the boiler returns to condensing efficiency, and you gain the headroom to run a lower flow temperature and a lower thermostat setting - together worth somewhere around £60 to £130 a year for a typical gas-heated home at current cap rates, and the comfort improvement arrives the same day. Bleed first, balance second, then turn the flow temperature down. Do those three in that order and you have captured most of the free savings available from a wet heating system without spending more than £25.

faq.q: What is the difference between bleeding and balancing radiators?
faq.a: Bleeding releases trapped air from inside a radiator so the whole panel fills with hot water; you do it at the small bleed valve at the top with a bleed key. Balancing adjusts the lockshield valve at the bottom of each radiator so hot water is shared fairly between all the radiators in the house. Bleed first, because air in the system makes balancing readings unreliable.

faq.q: Do I need to drain the heating system to balance radiators?
faq.a: No. Balancing only involves turning the lockshield valve spindles, which restrict flow without opening the sealed system. No water is released, no pressure is lost and no plumber is needed. The only time draining comes into it is if a lockshield valve is seized or leaking and has to be replaced.

faq.q: How often do radiators need balancing?
faq.a: Once done properly, balancing holds until something changes the system: a radiator added or removed, a new boiler or pump, a powerflush, or a valve being knocked or fiddled with. Most homes only need it redone every few years, if at all. Keeping a note of each lockshield position in turns from closed makes any future redo a ten-minute job.

faq.q: Can I balance radiators without a thermometer?
faq.a: Yes, roughly. Open everything fully, run the heating from cold, and progressively close the lockshields on the radiators that heat up fastest until all of them warm at about the same rate. It is less precise than measuring a 12°C flow-to-return drop with clip-on thermometers, but it still fixes the worst of the hot-hallway, cold-bedroom problem.

faq.q: Why is the radiator furthest from the boiler always the coldest?
faq.a: Hot water takes the easiest path, so radiators near the pump pass more flow and the furthest one gets whatever is left, having also lost heat along the pipe run. That is exactly what balancing corrects: throttling the lockshields on the nearby radiators pushes more flow to the end of the circuit, so the far radiator finally gets its share. Its own lockshield normally ends up fully open.
